item.title: Indi Endaya was here
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
I am a multi-disciplinary designer with a background in media, communication and culture. I enjoy bringing research to life visually and am experienced in crafting and enhancing content for various audiences and platforms; turning complex information into engaging and accessible stories and explaining technical issues to non-technical people. 

In my journey so far, I've had the pleasure of working with some amazing people: academics and policymakers in the UK; grassroots-based feminists and community radio broadcasters in the global south; teenagers learning how to write for the web; deaf students needing training in Photoshop; musicians, DJs, and artists all eager to share their valuable insights. 

I like learning new things and exploring new places. I am happiest when working in that sweet spot where technology, art and storytelling come together.

Elsewhere Online

(I was surprised to find bits of work from the distant past.)

CITATIONS

  • Marte-Wood, A.S. (2022). Domestic Shifts: Reproducing Peripheral Realism in Philippine Call-Center Fiction. ariel: A Review of International English Literature 53(4), 1-39. doi:10.1353/ari.2022.0031.
    "As one of the first works of Philippine call-center fiction, Endaya's Nick Joaquin Literary Award-winning short story "Shift" (1999), which was published in the esteemed University of the Philippines anthology series The Likhaan Book of Poetry and Fiction, poignantly contemplates the dissolution of human contact against the ironic backdrop of the BPO industry's attempt to valorize communication."
  • Recidoro, Zeny May D., Works-in-Progress: Artistic practices and digital communities (PDF), Writing Presently. Philippine Contemporary Art Network
  • Gonzales, Vladimeir B., Hyperwriting: Isang Walkthrough, Likhaan: the Journal of Philippine Contemporary Literature Vol. 3. 2009 pp. 206 – 216.

EXHIBITED WORK

  • NEW! “Life’s Missing Pushbuttons” and "Rapid Auntie Jen" by indicodeswitch. The Coding Train Passenger Showcase Highlight.
  • "Two young women migrants" Digital Art for Imagining ourselves: a global generation of women. International Museum of Women. Indira Endaya
  • “After the Curfew”. and “Batang Bhaktapur” shot in Kathmandu and remixed/rasticulated by Indi Endaya for the Babae sa Tag-araw group exhibit at Penguin Cafe, Remedios circle, Malate, Manila. 25 April-25 May 25, 2006.

PUBLICATIONS

  • Endaya, Indira Myra C, "Shift." The Likhaan Book of Poetry and Fiction, 1999, edited by Ricardo M. de Ungria and Jose Y. Dalisay, U of the Philippines P, 2001, pp. 139–51.
  • Endaya, Indira Myra C, “Nursing Lousy GenX Beer”  FutureShock Prose: An Anthology of Young Writers and New Literatures Edited by Ian Rosales Casocot. Sands and Coral 2002. The Literary Journal of the Silliman University Creative Writing Program
  • “Growing up in Studios” in Alter/(n)ations: The Art of Imelda Cajipe Endaya edited by Flaudette May V. Datuin University of the Philippines Press
  • Endaya, Indira Myra C “Of Hipsters and Hegemonies.”  Women in Action 2002-3, Io International Feminist Archives
  • Endaya, Indira Myra C, “Let the Critics Die with Envy” by Indira Endaya. June 2000  Manila Artist-Run Spaces Archive: Third Space, Asia Art Archive
  • Antonio, Bernardo, Endaya, Roque, Vasquez. The tongue-twister Effect. Subvocal Articulation in Silent Reading (PDF). Philippine Journal of Psychology. University of the Philippines, Volume 26, No. 1. June 1993

item.title: 100 days of code
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Update: I've added an Instagram link to the menu now. Yay!

Hello world!

This pandemic has seen me trying to regain a foothold on Web Dev, Modern Javascript, and Python1 then gravitating to the p5.js library and community. I have henceforth reactivated myself in other online spaces. (Let's see if I can actually get anything done.)

  • Substack - dispatches on my so-called coding journey plus a bunch of distractions that get me going along the way
  • Twitter - #100DaysOfCode
  • Github - slowly but surely
  • CodePen - I opened this previously when I was doing Freecodecamp exercises. I was able to go through HTML and CSS ones but got stuck on the certification tasks. I suppose I can go back to it.

The Coding Train and Computational Mama have brought me the most joy.

item.title: Southeast Asia International Affiars Programme Design Work
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded: Editor-in-Chief:  Dr Nicholas Kitchen Creative Director: Indira Endaya Format:  Print, Ebook, Web    Summary: Southeast Asian states risk becoming pawns in a geopolitical clash between two extra-regional superpowers. This report analyses how the states in the region are responding to the challenge posed by the strategic interests of the US and China in their geography and economy. If the Southeast Asian states are to take advantage of the opportunities presented by China’s rise and the United States’ pivot, they must stand together in the geopolitical contest currently taking place in the region. However, this is not an easy task: regional states are caught in what game theory would view as a classic ‘prisoners’ dilemma’ that will require a deep degree of trust to escape. IDEAS Special reports are research papers offering in-depth analysis. Includes special reports which feature a range of world-class expertise on vital issues and reports produced by IDEAS projects. (This report was being put together in the middle of the US election period which concluded in Obama's 2nd term. I really had to cross my fingers that the alternative cover featuring Mitt Romney would not be needed.)         Event posters:     Conference branding study:

item.title: Website mock-ups: PhD Hive Peer Review platform
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded: (Prototype stage) PhD Hive is an online platform that enables ​researchers to find reviewers specialised in their field, and obtain a detailed review in under 72 hours. It is an open and growing community where anyone can put themselves forward as a reviewer. However, ultimately it is user ratings that will determine how successful a reviewer is.

item.title: Research Centre Logo 1 and other new adventures in branding
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded: Logo and branding project request from the new Saw Swee Hock Southeast Asia Centre launching in January 2015.  Initial photo peg was the HK skyline which was replaced with the KL skyline.     The image of the skyline was too busy so I focused on the Southeast Asian boat, which was good and ripe with symbolism. The SSH-SEAC director loved the boat with the triangles. Yay! Next day he changed his mind.  There an issue with colours  (yellow conveyed different things in different Southeast Asian countries, changing it to red didn't help either.) So it was back to the drawing board. To cut a long story short, this eventually became the approved logo.  Since the colours were ditched, it would be pointless to use the modular triangles to form the S.  In the end, we were able to keep the boat and the letter S. Not all was lost. (Vector illustration done on Adobe Illustrator) Branding Guidelines  (sample spreads) Social Media mock-up

item.title: CSB Deaf Community's expo booth
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded: Set-up and design for the Deaf students showcase booth at the Panibagong Paraan Expo for Corporate Social Responsibility at the Megatrade Hall, Pasig City. The school  won a million pesos of project funding and the People's Choice award. From my previous incarnation as Media Specialist at the DLS-CSB School of Deaf Education and Applied Studies (SDEAS).  The kids I trained in Photoshop did the pictorial. Earnest and I did the promotional material. Visitors got to try out sign language and were rewarded with free brewed coffee, homemade chocolates, and learned some more deaf culture trivia.

item.title: Monty Passion
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Anybody else feel like a little... giggle... when I mention my fwiend... Biggus...

Michael Palin. IoE London Book Signing

I chanced upon Michael Palin's visit to our school last night to talk about his latest book, Diaries 1967-1979: The Python Years.  IoE's Logan Hall was packed to the rafters.

Mr. Palin, joking about how critics accused him of being conciliatory and "relentlessly nice," was as kind as he was funny and generous with the Python Years anecdotes. One time they were doing the "dead mother" sketch and the audience reception (from mayors and other major VIPs) turned out to be "the kind of silence you get when you're trying to record sounds in outer space."

He also talked about the difficulty of shooting that sequence of the "Knights who say 'Ni!' in Holy Grail:  up a step ladder with a long, heavy, trailing cloak in freezing, body-numbing, weather in Scotland, sapped of strength and a barely a squeak of a voice to yell 'Ni!';  in annoyance to lots of camera men perched on trees! 

Later, I had him sign a copy of Full Circle from his travel series.  He has this to say about the Philippines: "You don't have to be long in the country to appreciate that the jeepney expresses the Filipino spirit:  emotional, exuberant, celebratory; endearing and unwary.  We are following a truck marked 'Careful Movers', which lurches forward, its backdoor swinging wildly open."

cover photo remix: Life of Bryan x Photofunia
item.category: Notes from a digital culture flâneuse

item.title: Kathmandu art project for the Babae sa Tag-Araw Exhibit at Penguin Cafe
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
(above image remixed with PhotoMosh)

Penguin Cafe (Rockola) and Babae sa Tag-araw  
present the Exhibit Closing Program on 24 May 2006, Wednesday, 9:00 p.m.  

Performances by
Djembe Players and Dancers from Burkina Faso (a must-see)
Isha
Carol Bello
Cynthia Alexander (tentative)
Poetry by
Libay Linsangan Cantor
Yanna Verbo Acosta

Plus surprise guests/performers and music jam!
Open music jam and open mic poetry reading after the program follows.

BABAE SA TAG-ARAW photo exhibitors are:
Carolina Rodriguez Bello
Libay Linsangan Cantor
Aileen Familara
Indira Endaya

FREE ENTRANCE!

After the Curfew

After the Curfew. shot in Kathmandu and remixed/rasticulated by Indi Endaya for the Babae sa Tag-araw group exhibit at Penguin Cafe, Remedios circle, Malate, Manila. 25 April-25 May 25, 2006.

Bottom label-maker text: 'But we need this money to buy Babbage wire for a radio transmitter, sabi ng kasama kong geek sa batang namamalimos nung Tibetan new year. Charles Babbage! Father of the computer! sabi ng batang uhugin. Wow.'

Batang Bhaktapur (Bhaktapur kid)

Batang Bhaktapur, shot in Kathmandu and remixed/rasticulated for the Babae sa Tag-araw group exhibit at Penguin Cafe, Remedios circle, Malate, Manila. 25 April-25 May 25, 2006.

Bottom label-maker text: 'Her big sister Gihini of the Dalit was a flight attendant. I also want to fly, she said. Like your sister? I said. No. I want to fly the plane so you can touch the clouds in the sky.'

Thanks, Kids and Adam for the exhibit photos.
Hat tip to to Brendan G for sharing the best tool to do this.

Fancy seeing you here!

'We'll always have Paris' is such a cliche; I’ll always have Kathmandu, 2002. I suppose. The Yak and Yeti hotel. My first international conference. For a second, I thought I'd keep these moments for myself.

Photos © I Endaya, Kathmandu, 2002.

Stupa-hopping (Boudhanath, Swayambhunath) prayer flags, cheeky monkeys, the Kumari, the ascetics caked in mud. Seduced by souvenir-shop semi precious stones, spinning prayer wheels, pashminas. A dance-y way to nod your head.

Manisha was saying that in these parts you would be the rebel by NOT wearing a nose ring. Bespectacled Ganga at the radio station was explaining a painted depiction of the caste system. (Sherpa is a surname, so I learn. Also, avoid the tourist traps of Thamel!) Anita was talking about her Dalit friend who was trying to escape it all by pursuing a flight attendant career.

Tandoori, dahl bhat, high butter tea. Maoist rebels stopping folks at checkpoints for tarriffs and then issuing official receipts on the way to Anapurna. You had bunked with your dad who was in that Tibetan Buddhist phase back then (or is he, still?). Some kind of shared hostel room. Tropical low-land urban jungle spine-shivers. Wood-burning stoves for just a little bit more warmth by the bed. (Sixteen degrees in the giddy fog. Fair weather, friend!)

item.title: 100 Days of Code
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
This pandemic has seen me trying to regain a foothold on Web Dev, Modern Javascript, and Python.1 Hence am reactivating myself in other spaces. (Let's see if I can actually get anything done.)

  • Substack - dispatches on my so-called coding journey plus a bunch of distractions that get me going along the way
  • Twitter - I only ever use this to retweet funny stuff or for promos, make a customer query. I know I shouldn't make a new account but some people from a former life actually follow me and would be weirded out by code-oriented tweets.
  • Github - I opened this a while back only to look at some design tools but I hope to be using the repositories soon
  • CodePen - I opened this previously when I was doing Freecodecamp exercises. I was able to go through HTML and CSS ones but got stuck on the certification tasks. I suppose I can go back to it.

Am pretty sure I want to do something in the direction of creative coding while trying to master Javascript and do Front-end stuff. I also want to naturally ease back into responsive design and UX-ish things and acquire more information design skills.

So far I want to:

  • complete exercises from The Coding Train
  • be productive on GitHub
  • go with Aldo to the next MozFest
  • be qualified enough to volunteer and be part of a team doing good things
  • be able to immerse in coding a couple of good creative projects that tell stories visually (am inclined towards a cross between the CD-ROM content of the late 90s and some low-fi Indie Games with zine aesthetics and values that appeal to me) I know there's hope for me yet.
  • build a responsive and dynamic landing page from scratch without using Wordpress anymore
  • keep on blogging over at substack.

So let me end this post with a new beginning. Hello world!

1 With ZTM via codercoder as I was enticed by Andrei's road map, great collection of resources, success story blogs. So the plan is .js and its creative coding libraries then python, then the designer track which am giving a go later as I have a bit of design fatigue from work.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value:
Let's start from the very beginning. A very good place to start.

I'm publicly committing to the 100DaysOfCode Challenge starting today!
https://t.co/0EmK9Vuccn #100DaysOfCode

My roadmaps: CS50, freecodecamp, ZeroToMastery WebDev,

— indicodes (@IE62611037) October 24, 2021

item.title: Growing Up in Studios
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Written in 2007 for Alter/(N)ations the Art of Imelda Cajipe Endaya. University of Philippines Press (2010)

 

For the artist, love of the work has to come first.  Art is not a soft option, it is a lifetime's struggle. --Jeanette Winterson, Books n Riches        

When I first got a glimpse of My Mother and Me (acrylic and collage on canvas) in 2006, it became an instant favourite. It was both so like and unlike Imelda Cajipe-Endaya's previous works. Windows, textures, stark color contrasts. The living room of the house of holiday visits turned temporary shelter in Newburgh is depicted here. It is empty but with telltale signs of lives lived fully. A basket of yarn and a cane rest by the sofa. Snow-encrusted tree branches can be glimpsed outside. A cold winter in a place so far away from home is countered by the cheering presence of flowers. A meta-picture wall hanging of my mother as a small child, looking shyly at the camera and her own mother, my grandmother, young and smiling.

Although I have yet to see the more recent studio where she had painted this, I can imagine what it is like. Growing up accustomed to the smell of Grumbacher and linseed oil in the morning, I’ve observed how mama’s materials and media changed with the passing of time as she embarked on her artistic journeys: from her prints, to her canvasses and collages, to her larger-than-life oil-paintings and then the tiny delicate acrylic works on handmade paper, to her sculptures, watercolors, installations, digital art, organizing work, and curatorships.

If there’s anything I learned early on in life, it’s how not to be afraid to be untidy and get one’s hands dirty. The San Juan studio in the Seventies was a clunky clutter of iron plates, etching knives, paint rollers, a plexiglass acid bath tray to clean the plates, and a heavy metal etching press straight out of Victorian steampunk novels. It was the time her works like Piko (a Filipino version of hopscotch), Forefathers, Hinog sa Araw  (Sun-ripened) came out. Pretty good stimuli for developing one’s sense of wonder.

The studio was never off-limits to me as creating art and rearing children had neither separate work hours nor clearly-defined breaks. Apparently, I was also allotted a work area of my own. This is documented in an old slide, which shows a picture of me with my own little wooden blue table, my hands covered with flour from homemade Play-Doh or “Clay” dough. (Evidence from the same slide also shows that early on, a career in sculpture was not in the offing for me.)

Towards the Eighties, we moved to our own place in Parañaque. Despite being blessed with a spacious yard with a nipa play-hut and lots of trees, mama’s studio was still a fascinating space for us kids. It was the coolest place at the bottom of the house and from the concrete beams of the unfinished ceiling, my folks hung an indoor swing for my little sister. We even had our own children’s work desks that doubled up as my pretend-detective agency office.

And yet, it was a serious creative workspace which housed the aforementioned tubes of oil paint, along with tin cans full of paint brushes, vats and vats full of carpenters glue, finishes, varnishes and fixatives.

Now that I think about it, strict borders never existed at home. The whole house was a studio with mounted canvasses that would stretch across the entire dining area floor.

There were swatches and patches all over:  Lola Minggay’s (my great grandma‘s) crocheted doilies, veils and scapulars, Time magazine covers with missiles of the Reagan years, kitschy table cloths with bright red apples and flowers, old jeans, red papier mache horses from Paete. Pieces meant to be discarded, but brought to life with new meaning: Jesus as a laborer in jeans, a handmade clothes dummy on a used ironing board, a plaster-cast hand coming out of a broken microwave oven, art from a damaged computer mother board. There was also a collection of news clippings wars in the Middle East, the EDSA revolution, and Filipino diasporas (men in Saudi Arabia, women in Hongkong.)

Thus, another thing I learned early on was not to throw anything away as they could be either material or stimuli. Also then, I was already grasping how my mother’s art was a reaction to the times, as much as a reaction to society, domesticity and gender roles.

How does it feel to be the daughter of an artist? I’ve been asked. Not too different from the daughter of a dentist or a housewife or a grocer, I would have wanted to believe.

In hindsight though, I must admit that mama's rearing practice was a tinge more experimental, post-colonial, non-consumerist, and non-conformist.

It was an early childhood of no Barbies and no junk food. There was gallery-hopping instead of shopping. This was before the art galleries were conveniently located in malls. I remember the City Gallery in Luneta, also hanging out as a little kid at the loft of the Hiraya Gallery in Malate which is still around but feels smaller because we’ve grown up. There were exhibits and some short-lived disappointments for the lack of red dots (stickers that meant “sold) because non-ornamental, non-escapist, social-realist paintings never really sell like hot cakes at gallery openings.

Funnily, it was only my university years that I met people who more or less shared the same kind of background but they were more bohemian, more confident, less hesitant to express themselves, whether in art, music or in writing.

For a while, I did try to hide this side of myself while trying to fit into my Catholic private school for high school girls in Manila. I remember being an insecure adolescent worried about what classmates would think of our bedroom walls which, although they were painted red blue and yellow like the Philippine flag, were filled with Crayola graffiti, as my sister and I were free to doodle on them. Also, all our heavy books at home–dictionaries, the Britannica, the Encyclopedias of Western Artists, had a thin coat of dried-up white glue on the dust covers because they were used to weigh down the mixed media mounted on the canvasses.

Then again, a friend found it fascinating that the bulky black electric transformers at home were deliberately splotched with pink and yellow paint. A visual flaw morphed into something interesting. I’d like to think of it as my mother’s approach: this growing into uniqueness and embracing it but not being so full of the self.

My mother tells me that when I was a very small child, I thought it was the duty of all mothers to make art. Perhaps this is because she sensibly nurtures her art as a part of life instead of wrapping it up with the stereotypical artiste’s ego and putting it on a cocktail party-festooned pedestal.

The life of any artist could be easily romanticized by words but my mother was never self-conscious about being an 'artiste' and never had airs or a sense of entitlement after achieving so much. It was pure love of the work, no flamboyant diva ego, just being really professional, as one of the gallery owners told me before. There was just too much to do: she would just move on to the next project or help another group of people with theirs. Making more room for more art to thrive.

Now that I am miles away from home and far from all that is comforting and familiar, I find myself buying four little framed canvases, brushes, a palette knife, and some tubes of bright acrylic. When I set them up in the room, in an organized sort of clutter, I feel more at home. Some kind of nostalgia for mama’s studio.

item.title: Eddie Campbell, ComICA Fest
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded: "The simplest most everyday things are better than created extravaganzas..." 2009 ComICA Festival, London.  The session with Eddie Campbell at the Institute of Contemporary Arts began at 7 and I was running late. (A long story involving a broken printer, Tube closures, busted trains, Neanderthal passengers, a wailing baby, a tsunami of Piccadilly Circus tourists and a hundred meter dash up numerous flights of stairs. If I had half the brilliance of Mr. Campbell, I would show all this in a chuckle-inducing 9-panel spread, but I digress.) By the time I make it to the lecture room, witty banter’s in full swing as high caliber Glaswegian comic book artist gets grilled by high caliber Glaswegian comic. It couldn’t be more apt. So there he was, Eddie Campbell, Alec MacGarry’ himself(!), in the flesh (plain undershirt, salt 'n' pepper hair). Having the piss taken out of him by comedian Arnold Brown for name-dropping Quentin Tarantino’s mum who once commissioned the former to sketch her own Bacchus, god of wine.  (Eddie:  Connie, yeah, she gets around…Arnie:  My God, how famous!) The main thread of the conversation here of course, would be the launch of Campbell’s mega-collection, Alec:  The Years Have Pants. A hefty 640-pager that brings together his semi-autobiographical Alec books–The King Canute Crowd, Graffiti Kitchen, How to be an Artist, Little Italy, The Dead Muse, The Dance of Lifey Death,  After the Snooter and some previously unpublished fragments plus a new book that bears the compilation’s title. Aldo had given me this wonderful omnibus as a birthday present and I was chuffed to lug it all the way to Central London to have it signed. Back in the early Nineties, I had stumbled upon Campbell’s autobiographical Alec stories while rummaging through Luis Katigbak's incredible stash of Book Sale finds. Barely held together by a Scotch-taped plastic bag, stripped of covers, and looking the worse for wear, those pages of candid, humorous stories brought to life with doodle-like yet quite refined drawings of Alec making funny faces with his new baby certainly caught my fancy. The storytelling was light and yet it tugged at the heartstrings. Not long after that, I came across Mr. Campbell’s name again as he gained more recognition for working with Alan Moore on From Hell. And by the time discussions on the graphic novel and sequential art ensued on local fanboy mailing lists, anyone with the money could soon get spanking-new, even signed copies of Campbell’s Top Shelf releases imported by high-end bookstores in Manila. Anyway, back to the conversation in London. If I had to tell you some highlights of what my muddled brain picked up from this whole funny, inspiring event (aside from a prized autograph and sketch, of course) it would all really boil down to these: Some tips on how to be an artist like Eddie Campbell 1.    Unassuming material is good material. Arnold Brown interrogates Campbell about the authenticity of his characters and stories.   Is there a real Danny Grey? Did he really leave you sleeping at the turnpike (as in the early King Canute comics)? We can easily guess the answer to that. Campbell nods and later cites more instances of art imitating life–in one of them he is fearless and twenty-three, pissing on the Thames from a rubber dinghy. Later, he shares that the $4000 he paid a travel agent for his recent Italian trip somehow got siphoned off, credit-crunch style, before the actual ticket was procured.  “There’s a story angle there and that would be good for my next book which is going to be all about money - the awful, horrid stuff!” How do you create things to write about? Someone in the audience asks Campbell who answers that there is no need (for him) to create things to write about. “Unassuming material is good indeed. The simplest most everyday things are better than created extravaganzas. Waking up in the middle of the night in cold sweat - now that already offers a million possibilities.” 2.   Imagine you are the Duke of Wellington. Just as it was for his alter ego Alec, (in King Canute #1) formal education was an obstacle to Eddie Campbell's artistic progress. He had quit his foundation course at what was then known as the Central School of Art and Design in London, and then spent five years in a factory, cutting sheet metal into squares. Did you take the job as an artist’s decision? Brown asks. Apparently, he did and the mindlessness of the job left him lots of room to read and ruminate on logic and mythology. “The important thing is that to be an artist you have to imagine you are one already.”  He smiles. “I thought I was a successful artist.  If anyone pointed it out that I wasn’t, I’d be crestfallen. In Glasgow, when I was 6, I thought I was a cowboy–not that I wanted to be one (mind,) but that I was one. To survive, you have got to be mad.  You have got to not notice you’re not getting respect. You gotta be oblivious to that. I thought I was the Duke of Wellington.” 3.  Sometimes the dining table can be better than a studio. A guy sitting across the aisle asks, “Do you still do your work on the dining table?”  Campbell shrugs and confesses that these days, he spends more work time in front of the computer. But he does wax nostalgic about that long kitchen table he used to toil on.  There was a demarcation line, he recalls, but paintbrushes would fall over the eating area. Zip-a-tones (those now-obsolete flat sticky sheets with dots and patterns used for shading in comics) were all over the place so much that. “If you walked around the house they would end up in the crack of your bum…heck and on the baby–in the folds of his neck.” “How can you work in this madness?” his wife had exclaimed. Eventually she convinced him to go work in a studio, sans the bedlam and the kids. And boy was it quiet in that studio.  Campbell laughs. “And what would you do in that silence?  I read a book.” “I need the noise to work.  All that alien information is good for creative thinking.  Natural thinking, as Edward de Bono calls it. (“The dullest stuff!  Self-help books.  Shouldn’t everyone think in the same way you used to think when you were three?”  On that note, Brown brings up Malcolm Gladwell’s The Outliers and his notion of devoting ten years to master a craft.) 4.    The god of wine is in the details. Brown praises Campbell’s eye for detail. “You have a laser eye – not a lazy eye.” And talk of technique ensues.  “The artist mind locks information in. Mentally sketching all the time.” Campbell says like a sage. Contrary to viewing cartoons as (simplified images) distilled from reality, Campbell shares that in painting Bacchus (the bespoke one for Tarantino’s mum) he had a really hard time getting the hair and whiskers right. How would you draw the hair of someone who was 4000 years old?  (In the end, he, uh, reveals that what he used for reference was pubes.) As for them zip-a-tones (which you can still find in Japan) he says someone sent him some and that he is “using it in a painterly way, like blobs of toothpaste. It looks juicy and painterly–that’s the word we artists like.” The cover of The Years Have Pants depicts Alec lying on grass-like scraps of Zip-a-tone.  The guy seated beside me comments that the cover reminds him of a funeral. “As if I float off to Avalon!”  Campbell light up.  Incidentally, he tells us that the cover designer is Eric Skillman who also designs Criterion Collection DVDs. 5.  A little humour won’t hurt "How would you know what to tell and what not to tell?" A guy at the back row enquires. Campbell says it was never really a major problem. He never got into deep trouble with friends or family on how they were being depicted in his stories. The Jester, the fool in society is after all, allowed to tell the truth, he said. He mentions Joe Sacco, another graphic novelist whom he thinks epitomises just that. “Now he is a handsome chap but always depicts himself as a foolish-looking character in order to talk about the Palestine and Israel conflict....The mix of humour in comics develops into the very root of what we are. Sacco can get away with tackling delicate situations regular journalists cannot talk about. 6.  Appreciate your peers Is there anyone out there that you think might push the media further? Someone in the audience asks. Campbell says he likes Craig Thompson, especially for his new book Carnet de Voyage, about his travels in Europe. He also mentions that he likes how the Blankets author draws figures "with lovely big feet."  Too bad someone told him to draw them smaller. Another new favourite is Robert Crumb’s Genesis. He singles out the part in which the Household of Abraham was told to get a circumcision and how in that one, Crumb’s eyes kept looking surreptitiously from side to side, as if to say, Do we have an option here? Everyone laughs. 7.     Keep on working and collaborating Eddie Campbell’s next project is The Playwright, written by Darren White, to be published by Top Shelf and Knockabout next year.  “It’s about the sex life of a celibate middle-aged man and there’s a lot of self-abuse in it.  Critics said ‘it’s not worth reading, it’s just full of wanking." We said–that should be the blurb!” You seem quite happy, Brown observes aloud to the artist, who doesn’t disagree. Ah, the actual Dance of Lifey Death. Eddie Campbell must know himself very well:  “Youth, old age, perfection, flaws, it’s all there in a big soup–a big Campbellian soup.”

item.title: Support displaced families
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Happy to be tapped by my friend Paola to help out with this craft fair poster in the early days of The Welcome Committee. 

We're a group of concerned Londoners doing something positive as a community to support refugees. We are doing this through sponsoring two Syrian refugee families to settle in London - giving them a fresh start, the chance to rebuild their lives in peace and hopefully have a new place they can call home. These refugee families have been identified by the UN Refugee Agency (UNHCR) and the Home office as in need of resettlement and the Home office select the families for us. Once they arrive, they will have official refugee status.

The Welcome Committee will be doing everything from finding the family members a place to live, to getting their medical checks, setting up bank accounts, helping them learn English, getting the kids into school - meeting all of their needs as much as we can for two years. We hope that this will give them the best chance of really settling into life in London.

This (above) poster was cobbled together from free online assets and customised with a lot of tweaks for a charity event organised by the Welcome Committee who work to support refugee families from Syria who have just begun new lives in London.

For the actual event, it was an honor to somehow share the solidarity with internally displaced persons from Marawi through zines and recipes from Gantala Press from the Philippines.

Lucky supporters in London had the chance to get a sneak preview of two publications:  MANGA TUTUL A PALAPA: Recipes and Memories from Ranao Assad Baunto; and  LAOANEN: Kababaihan / Digmaan / Kapayapaan by Faye Cura.
item.category: Notes from a digital culture flâneuse

item.title: Lockdown Learning: Kinetic Type 1
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
I've started this Domestika online course on Kinetic Type and here is my first output, warts and all. The lesson's in Spanish but the bigger challenge for me is trying to get used to the finer details of AfterEffects and all these fiddly layer configurations to make masks into paths for the text to move on. Now am trying to think of a smarter use for making type move in a  circle.

Fun things that come to mind: Dante's Nine Circles of  Hell,  Lord of the Rings rings, Camera Lens, Solar systems, Orbitals, Kaleidoscopes,

Anyway this one has two song excerpts in it. Bachelorette by Bjork and Windmills of Your Mind by Michel Legrand1

The centre of the circle features a Vegvísir2 a Norse sigil supposedly meant to protect its owner from rough weather. (Am aware of the resemblance to Greendale Community's motto or Kurt Vonnegut's asterisk*.)

1When I was around 6 my mom got me a book written by her favourite French composer called Michel's Mixed-up Musical Bird which I only discovered was made into a film https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1JzbmH9t4Gc which I shall force my kid to watch with me.)

2Incidentally, this is something I also remember from the Nineties as the design of Bjork's tattoo.

Kinetic Type 2: Stretch it!

These are the ways am trying not to be sedentary during this planetary house arrest: 200 piddly kilocals on Aldo's water rower. 30 minutes of Box VR with music I hate on the Oculus Quest and joining Juju for his Online Learning PE Class, trying to keep up with Joe Wicks who's managing to do squats and burpees in a froggie outfit.  And the sanctioned walk to the grocer's on the high street.

I never thought I would actually miss going to the gym.

Font: Coolvetica
MOOC: Domestika
Software: AfterEffects
BGHex: 93B4CB

item.title: Identity Pony Tricks
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Note: Post under revision while auditing the HarvardX MOOC, Tangible Things: Discovering History Through Artworks, Artifacts, Scientific Specimens, and the Stuff Around You

Illustration © Indira Endaya

My image reference: An old miniature takâ, a quirky souvenir which I rediscovered under layers of dust in the bedroom of the house I grew up in. I somehow managed to stick this little red horse into my suitcase before flying back to London in 2018. (Someone told me to bring back Red Horse beer but this was even better.) The keepsake is time-stamped with hand-painted letters: 'December 30, 2001. Keith and Issa.’

Back in the day, I attended this out-of-town wedding with a friend and his family. I remember happily chatting with GCF whose car it was that took us on that long drive from Quezon City to the bride’s ancestral house in the lakeside town of Pakil. After the vows were exchanged in church, the town plaza pretty much turned into an arts fest with at least two generations of artists (sculptors, art film auteurs, new-age earth mamas, painters, poets, literal drama queens) invited to join in the dancing as a traditional marching band played. Fiesta food was laid out on outdoor tables which were set to include the special touch of the complimentary miniature red horses for us guests to take home.

Seven years after that, in another part of the world, I encountered another red horse. At the Ikea in Croydon, of all places. There it was, emblazoned on a huge banner hanging from two stories above a showcase of assembled flatpack furniture. Complete with white harness and handprinted floral decorations. No way, I said to myself. A huge promotional image of the good old takâ in Ikea?! I started to wonder how they poached the design then had it mass produced. Then thoughts of the effects of capitalism on small communities trickled in.

My original plan was to just take the tram home and enjoy the Färgrik mugs that sold for less than a pound; not to think about all the cultural appropriation baggage that came with what the banner advertised. Not to start mourning lost childhood memories of road trips to the woodcarving village of Paete in Laguna de Bay. Homesickness is so easy to trigger. Later, back in the flat, I did a bit of Googling and learned that as a matter of fact, the red horse is actually an iconic piece of folk art found all over Sweden, a national icon called the Dala Horse.

A Dalecarlian horse or Dala horse (/ˌdɑːləˈkɑːrliən/;[1] Swedish: Dalahäst) is a traditional carved, painted wooden statue of a horse originating in the Swedish province of Dalarna (Dalecarlia). In the old days the Dalecarlian horse was mostly used as a toy for children; in modern times it has become a symbol of Dalarna, as well as of Sweden in general….One particular style has, however, become much more common and widespread than others. It is stoutly carved and painted bright red with details and a harness in white, green, yellow and blue.

—https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dalecarlian_horse

Ok It looks remarkably similar but it isn’t the same. It’s made of wood, not papier-mâché, I tell myself. However, I find that it dates back to the 16th century and it wouldn’t be a stretch of the imagination to say it could probably even be where the takâ got its look. For some reason, this compelled me to gather more intel online.

Old folks claim that Mexican friars introduced takâ-making to the pueblo of Paete some centuries ago as a cottage industry for the female population while the males worked on woodcarving.

—https://traveleronfoot.wordpress.com/tag/papier-mache-of-paete/

The Paeteños believe that the idea originated in Mexico with a significant difference to what we have in Paete. While the Mexican “pinata” is decorated with cut-off colored paper, the Paete’s takâ are hand-painted and are sometimes small enough for little girls to use  as dolls.

According to the Paete Phenomenon exhibited in the Bulwagang Juan Luna at the Cultural Center of the Philippines, Maria Bague made the first known taka in 1920s. It was wrapped around a mold carved from wood and painted with decorative pattern that eventually became toys and ornaments. Unfortunately, nobody knows if Maria Bague’s taka ever existed as her town was destroyed by fire and almost all traces of the takas she produced have vanished.

—PAETE’S TAKA by: Mailah Baldemor

Taka was pioneered by Paete local, Maria Piday. During Christmas, Piday was in charge of the church's decorations. The wooden angels and cherub was heavy causing the carvings to fall. Piday devised the lightweight taka paper mache as an alternative to the wooden sculptures. Piday was also a maker of local toys such as the yoyo and the small acrobat hand puppet. Taka eventually became folk art and was sold to nearby towns for festivals. In the 1970s, Tere Afuang, a knowledgeable practitioner of the craft, popularized the craft.

Due to exposure and migration of Paete residents to Manila and abroad, European- influenced paper mache toys began to be made for export to other countries, such as Germany. Taka images now include those of Santa Claus, reindeer, giraffes, and other subjects that are in demand.

—https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taka_(paper_mache)

This Maria Piday/Maria Bague: had she perhaps stumbled upon a galleon-trade wooden horse with a Spanish-Swedish provenance? Then maybe she used it as a base for the cast and painted it red but in her own floral style similar to jeepney art? Hmmm.

I asked Mama about it. A child of true Paetenians, she was quite protective of the takâ heritage as I expected and thought a Swedish connection was a bit far-fetched. She then suggested that it could be a case of repetitive patterns in anthropology; archetypes. On whether horses are indigenous to the Laguna area, she said she didn’t exactly know but weren't T’bolis known to make earrings from horsehair and wasn't Gabriela Silang supposed to have ridden a horse in Pangasinan? Also there seems to be no available research on the export of takâ horses as most of our local history seems to be oral-based.

I could only find Western pieces of scholarship on the net mentioning Swedish identity and exported dala horses from Southeast Asia. It was supposed to include a picture of the takâ and the Dala horse which was not available online but the notes say:

“The similarity of the two horses calls to mind Hitchcock’s (1998, p132) reporting of a version of a Bavarian castle in the Indonesian village museum of Taman Mini.”

Hanefors, Monica & Selwyn, Tom. (2019). Dalecarlian Masques: One Souvenir’s Many Voices. 10.4324/9781315187457-19.

Wait, so what I thought was a heritage craft is just cheap replica? The colonial chip on my shoulder hurts, man.

There must be something there. Can of worms? Unturned stone? All ripe for my future speculative historical literary work to emerge (haha).

So many stories that are not part of the legitimised narrative. So many in-betweens.

Their conclusion though, had the same kinds of questions I always asked:

“The coming of globalisation, as several disguises (the Philippine and Danish Horses, the cast of Rinkeby horses, the Horse as kangaroo, for example) indicated, raised many kinds of interrelated questions about national identity. What does globalisation do to countries, their myths, legends, and the identities of their populations?…Are Philippine ritual artefacts as transient as the Dala Horse itself seems to be? How can A Gambian or Columbian immigrant become Swedish? and so on.           

Hanefors, Monica & Selwyn, Tom. (2019). Dalecarlian Masques: One Souvenir’s Many Voices. 10.4324/9781315187457-19.

Takâ, is neither a Swedish Dalecarlan horse nor a Mexican piñata. Perhaps it could be a mix of both and I might have happily lost the plot here because the point may no longer be about provenance but innovation, borders and cross-cultural pollination. It’s certainly another signifier to add to the collected narratives on Filipino national identity. (Just like the jeepney, the kalesa, San Miguel Beer, Mexican Mangoes that came from the Philippines via the galleon trade, the karaoke and the yo-yo origin story.)

So. Here's to the non-essentialist, the post-colonial, the outsourced, the innovative, the repurposed folk arts and crafts. (Note to self: revisit old texts post-Benedict Anderson and Edward Said then spike this with a dash of Mark Fisher on consciousness-raising, ha!)

Further links that reminded me of this object:

  • a talk by the artist Jenny Odell, noting that the Sampaguita, the national flower of the Philippines actually came from the Himalayas
  • a post on Rene Redzepi’s instagram where I learned that the Mexican mango came from the Philippines during the galleon trade
  • tinikling dance performed by Chinese school kids
  • the film Turumba
  • Image of the Manila Sarao jeepney labelled as part of amazing decorated trucks of India

References:

Monica Hanefors & Tom Selwyn. (2019). Dalecarlian Masques: One Souvenir’s Many Voices. 10.4324/9781315187457-19.

Mark Fisher, K-Punk: The Collected and Unpublished Writings of Mark Fisher (2004–2016), ed. Darren Ambrose, London 2018.

item.title: Attention on Children
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Logo design project for a series of Children's Community Theatre Workshops in Poland

NOTES:

Uwaga dzieci. Note that the word na is added to make the sentence work (originally it's "Attention children" but should be "Attention on children."

"Watch Out for Children" (the words would have to be in Polish "Uwaga na Dzieci".

Special Requests:

  • to base it on a well-known street sign (the equivalent of "Watch out for Children")
  • to have a strong, simple design
  • to attract the attention of children so that they will come and take part in the workshops
  • to feature children
  • have a symbol of theatre within it (e.g. Greek theatre comedy and tragedy masks)

PROCESS:

Many elements were developed from the initial image of the street sign

  • The triangle  has evolved into a spotlight beaming down on the stage in a dark theatre. It still retains the yellow and dark hazard stripes colour scheme of signs.
  • There are two kids representing the concept of a community. I've kept two since adding many human figures would make it too busy to fit in a single logo (which would have to be reduced to fit various small materials such as stationery and business cards etc.)
  • The joyful spirit is there and I've done away with actual masks but there is a hint of them with the happy face on the left and the upside down smile on the right.
item.excerpt:encoded: Logo design project for a series of Children's Community Theatre Workshops in Poland

item.title: La Festa di sant'Efisio
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Photo taken at the Festival of St Efisio, Cagliari, Sardinia.

Celebrating the 17th Century Plague's End

If only there was a way to ward it all off. What would you promise to do in exchange for making a terrible chain of events disappear?

The people of 17th-century Sardinia held this procession to honor a certain saint who supposedly wiped out the plague:

It was in 1652 that Sardinia suffered with the plague and much of the population died. In their desperation, the people turned to Saint Efisio in order to try and free the island from the disease. Efisio - a Roman Commander - had been sent by Emperor Diocletian to suppress Christianity in Sardinia, but upon landing on the island and experiencing what’s described as an epiphany, he converted to Christianity. In 303 AD, Efisio was imprisoned in Cagliari, and shortly after made a martyr by being beheaded by the Romans on the beach of Nora after refusing to deny his new, Christian faith.

The Sardinian people vowed that if Saint Efisio were to rid them of their terrible plague; they would march in an elaborate procession in his honour each year. 1656 finally saw the end of the plague, and true to their pledge, every year since that day the people of Cagliari have taken to the streets to perform an extravagant parade in an array of traditional clothing to celebrate the Saint.

--Sardinian Places

Four Centuries later, here's a photo caption from my mobile phone.

Throwback Thursday. April 2016*. What's off the frame:  red-pink-white rose petal showers, town piazza by the food stalls: a casu marzu (maggot cheese) monger in medieval shepherd's garb, paper ticket stubs, wooden bleachers, girls in lace (false eyelashes, crimson gloss-lipped), marching bands, hymns to a wooden saint, cattle-drawn carriages festooned with the harvest of the season: (artichokes and leafy greens? I vaguely remember) disappearing into the shadows of The Golden Arches of McDonald's, Cagliari.

Four years forward, celebrating the end of the plague is a dream.

item.title: Book cover design concept
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Governing the Global Drug Wars edited by Collins & Soderholm

Content summary:

Since 1909 the international community has worked to eradicate the abuse of narcotics. A century on, the efforts are widely acknowledged to have failed, and worse, have spurred black market violence and human rights abuses. How did this drug control system arise, why has it proven so durable in the face of failure, and is there hope for reform?

item.title: Riso and shine
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
After a few years of checking out all those Pick me Up graphic design festivals at Somerset House, collecting piles of art school degree show print samples, and admiring artwork featured in graphic design exhibits (similar to the Rise of Riso, as photographed above, although that came much later) I was left with the impression that Risography, which I used to write off as a niche retro-artisanal tool (similar to lomo cameras or 8-bit pocket synthesizers) was already 'having its own smashed avocado moment' way before the green stuff caught on with the brunch crowd this side of the prime meridian.

Being rather more curious than trend-averse, not to mention starved for some creative release (not involving any kind of avocado whatsover), I signed up for a Risograph printing class held at the basement of my favourite new bookshop in the East End.

After work, I took the tube to Aldgate East and finally got to see what was at the bottom of the staircase at Libreria.

Actually, the Risograph machine itself was not really a sight to behold.

It was after all, originally meant to be a digital duplicator: an ink-plus-stencil alternative to the more conventional toner-plus-heat dependent laser printers and photocopiers. But as Jess, our mentor and resident print specialist soon showed us, clichés also apply to copiers: ie, it's what's inside that matters. For indeed, beneath the ‘greige’ office copier exterior, sat these Willy Wonka-esque cylinders of teal, aqua blue, fluoro pink ink: the source of the rich spot colours that make Riso so distinct, so extra. That you can layer these colours on top of each other to produce new ones is a game changer too.

Apparently riso is more about ink, ink, ink, and less about rendering very fine details (a struggle to do with stencils, right.) Ink takes longer to dry on paper and this leads to unpredictable results; bugs turned into features: constructive flaws that lend art editions that handmade, 'no two are alike', silkscreen print look and feel.

As we signed up for a collage-based poster project, piles of old indie magazines were sacrificed so we could stick interesting cutout compositions on tracing paper. Those would then be run through the machine and transformed into a master stencil, one master stencil per layer of colour.

I had a bit of a relaxed start with ice-cold beer and Six Music playing in the background while the other learner in the room, this young woman who said she worked for a startup, was already making these funky Monstera Deliciosa leaf shapes.

Skimming through the glossy assortment, I soon landed on a page with various shots of David Bowie in the Seventies and excitedly tore off the page that featured him in that shiny stripy Kansai Yamamoto jumpsuit. A proper icon to work around.

Then things just chirpily fell into place. The collage was done and with much support and expertise from Jess, I was able to come up with my very own A3 print editions. A numbered series of 15.

That was so satisfying. I definitely enjoyed the process. To top that all off, I even got a discount card to use for the bookshop upstairs. Yay!

No time is too soon to do this again. Next time I'll do a zine or maybe some Christmas cards.

Interesting links on riso:

House of the Risograph
Hato Press
Risotto Studio (Scotland)
Studio Fludd
Duplikat Press
The Rise of Riso

item.title: The Scratch That Won't Be Itched
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
'kinda blue'. photo and words © 2010 Indira C. Endaya

originally submitted as part of a collection edited by Luis Katigbak

I was in the aftermath of some seriously deep shit the first time M called out of the blue when I thought we were finally a continent apart. I remember walking aimlessly into some random North London park, settling on a bench and staring in fascination at some Orthodox Jewish boys - in their three-piece black suits and skull caps - playing badminton in the summer heat.

“Am in town, Wanna talk, drink or eat?” M tweeted.

I could never say no. Old habits and all.

We met up at the Britannia Gagosian where there was a JG Ballard tribute exhibit (not my idea) and moved to a pub in the area (my idea) to wash down the lurid aftertaste of some displayed surgical instruments and hi-res photos of crashed pilot body parts. 

That was when she started telling me that she had overstayed her residence visa and that she was on the run.

“Immigration storm trooper types were all over the underground last night. The trick here is to never bat an eyelash, be as deadpan as can be. Earphones are always handy so don’t run or act suspicious if they do the stop and search thing.... “

“What are you talking about? I thought you had the right to remain after that scholarship grant?”  But she wasn’t even listening.

“What I know is that they have no right to ask for documentation or biometric crap. Just basic ID stuff. So I hope you don’t mind that I gave them your name, post code, date of birth.    

“You what? ??!"

We were the same age, same racial profile. And now the same side of the binary tick-box. How convenient. My old flame and I have become one, finally. Never imagined it this way though.  

She was lucky to do that during an era of flawed face recognition and biometric fingerprint software which she was fully geared for then, before Home Office went full on with the DNA database crap. 

“They did take note but they let me go. So if they get back to you – you know where you were….Anyway, we all look alike to them.” She said just like that, like it was a minor inconvenience.

***

“Am in town, Wanna talk, drink or eat?” Our second meal here in the EU was a decade later over curry wurst and shared swigs of Bruichladdich, beside that Kaiser Wilhelm Bombed-out church in Berlin.

M had just told me that her current partner was spear-fishing somewhere in what used to be Palawan and getting the full rock star treatment from the locals. Things never change, really. 

 “Oh Fortress Europe,” she started addressing the structure, oblivious to the stares of tourists passing by.

“Let’s all raise a toast to this maximum surveillance museum we have learned to call home.” The street food was cheap but the whiskey she brought along was Nikka, 51.4% strong.

“Fortress…”  I found myself muttering. “Your citizens are well-preserved and still bored enough to seek some orientalist adventure in the place we grew up.”

Then she brought out some leftover space cakes which were back in fashion as were the coffeehouse revivals. They were lousy and I refused to take more than the first bite which simultaneously managed to loosen my tongue and leave that swimming in syrup sensation of sports event reruns in slo-mo each time I had to turn my head.

“Blast from the past! Did I ever tell you what happened after you left London? Did you ever wonder if they knocked on my door after stopping you on the tube?” This church can survive more bombs.

She gave me a puzzled look like it was a vague memory to her.

“They never asked me where I was, but other kinds of authorities did. It happened on the same evening of the lowest point in my life and you gave me the best alibi ever. The records maintain that I was stopped on the tube and this happened at the same time that I almost killed some knife-wielding dickhead, trying to defend myself.”

“Wow.” she said while flicking off crumbs from her layers of clothes.  

Maybe I wasn’t that coherent.  Maybe she wasn’t even capable of listening in that state.  Fine with me. At least that was out, though many things were still left unsaid between us.

***

“Am in town, Wanna talk, drink or eat?”  Meeting number three, I forget the year, found us both homesick for the tropics as we lingered in the Southeast Asian section of the Aarhus Botanical Gardens with its thermostat jacked up in the middle of the Danish winter. Memories of ex-homelands triggered by the smell of Philippine soil and the scent of dalandans turning ripe in the precisely configured humidity.   

Then she told me she volunteered to go through the controversial Longevity™ treatments, before everyone, including holdouts, (like me) decided it wasn’t a bad idea.

“Remember going to the orthodontist for tweakings? That’s all there is to it. Many visits to a clinic won’t actually make you feel a major transformation is taking place until you’re done. Oh and the pill diet, of course.” 

M was always the early adopter.

“But why, really? What’s the point?  Why choose to live long in this kind of world?” I remember asking her after a beat.

“I am scared to die without having changed the world.” M replied.

“How utterly…. Romantic, whatever that means.”

And that was the last thing I said to her, at that last time we both agreed to meet. 

It’s been years, and eventually I found myself riding what became the bandwagon of Longevity™. I guess I was just scared to die cynical and numb. I had no longer had her grand dreams to change a world that had already changed me. And yet.

Why am I still holding my breath?
item.category: Notes from a digital culture flâneuse

item.title: 18 years ago. Crumbs!
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
It’s been 18, yes, 18(!!!!) years since Crumbs. This independent collaborative online project with Adam, was partly borne out of the frustration we had working for a well-funded startup that was supposed to empower teens but was actually kind of really run by their parents. A lo-fi anomaly in the golden age of Macromedia Flash, Crumbs was pretty well-received and even got actual link-shares from websites which mattered a lot to me back then (Metafilter, links.net! God am old.)

What I'd give to have the same level of energy--and inspiration and delight in sharing small things that brought personal joy-- that just flowed when tweaking things for this labour of love back in the day. It was our own way of presenting our stories, for those who'd care to read them. It was also a gushy pixelated showcase of various things that we chose to obsess about back then. I remember staying up all night with the most twee mp3s (Eggstone!) playing in the background while tinkering with bootlegged Photoshop files on a dying monitor (hence the strange washed-out color scheme for most of my graphics) and then looking for free Javascript libraries to mesh with Dreamweaver to create virtual magnetic fridge magnets that would pop out personal stories if you clicked on certain tiles.

Years after, I was tickled, okay, a bit thrilled to hear from afar that Crumbs had been studied academically as an example of Philippine hypertext literature in English, our own nifty little contribution to the digital landscape of that era when the world wide web was still a good place to be.

Related Links:

  • Recidoro, Zeny May D., Works-in-Progress: Artistic practices and digital communities (PDF), Writing Presently. Philippine Contemporary Art Network
  • Gonzales, Vladimeir B., Hyperwriting: Isang Walkthrough, Likhaan: the Journal of Philippine Contemporary Literature Vol. 3. 2009 pp. 206 – 216.
  • Casocot, Ian Rosales. FutureShock Prose: An Anthology of Young Writers and New Literatures

item.title: Lockdown Learning: Into the uncertain future
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Part of a series of exercises in using Adobe After Effects and Cinema 4D to animate Typographic Designs.

Locked down and continuing to educate myself in creating Kinetic Typography (with a Spanish teacher, no less.) The FLOATing graphic you see above is actually the result of hours of getting used to one of the fiddliest dashboards I've worked with so far.

Exercise 5: The infinite wave in Cinema 4D Lite.

Achievement unlocked:

  • Use the wind transformer in Cinema 4D to give an extruded typeface a movement similar to waves on the sea

Exercise 6: Typographic Twist

Achievement unlocked: To twist your in Cinema 4D using a deformator

NOTE: 'Pilipit' means twisted in Filipino

I would like to do all this one day without proprietary software

Achievements unlocked:

  • Use the lathe spline to create a black hole in Cinema4d;
  • Animate Photoshop generated text through this surface; and

item.title: Motherland '22
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
This creative coding exercise to express online solidarity with the Pink Campaign during the Philippine Presidential Elections is an application of a sketch from the book Generative Design.

Image is a derivative work with code from the book Generative Gestaltung. Copyright 2018. http://www.generative-gestaltung.de Generative Gestaltung Hermann Schmidt, Mainz, 2018 Benedikt Groß, Hartmut Bohnacker, Julia Laub, Claudius Lazzeroni with contributions by Joey Lee and Niels Poldervaart. Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License"); you may not use this file except in compliance with the License. You may obtain a copy of the License at http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0 Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS, WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied. See the License for the specific language governing permissions and limitations under the License.

Photo reference via Official Gazette PH.

VISUAL TREATMENT:

pixel mapping. each pixel is translated into a new element (letter)1

Click the image then try:

KEYS

1 : toggle font size mode (dynamic/static)
2 : toggle font color mode (color/b&w)
arrow up/down : maximal fontsize +/-
arrow right/left : minimal fontsize +/-
s : save png

FULL TEXT: Leni Robredo’s declaration of 2022 presidential bid from Rappler

[translated from Filipino]...We should all know by now: There is much hunger, suffering, and death because those in power have prioritized self-interest over the welfare of the Filipino. The lack of good governance lies at the root of our many problems. This needs to end.

And if we truly want to liberate ourselves from this situation, we should change not just the surnames of those in power; the corruption, the incompetence, the lack of compassion must be replaced by competence and integrity in leadership. We should be prepared to take out entirely the agendas, the interests, the very people and kind of politics that is the root of all that we are going through right now. If you will not take a clear line; if you are willing to compromise; if you can’t even say that what is wrong is wrong – then whose side are you really on?

Where I stand is clear: I am on the side of all who are doing what they can to lift themselves, their families, and their fellow human beings from this pandemic. Though our contexts may be different, the feeling of being in the margins should be familiar to all – when you grasp for a lifeline, when you just want to knock on any door, when you cry out to whomever might be able to hear: Help.

item.title: Art Fan Art 1.
item.dc:creator: thirstyfish@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Using creative coding to recreate the work of South Asian Womxn Artists

Am happy to share a selection of my project sketches from the wonderful workshop series by computational_mama. These were efforts to recreate art from Rana Begum, Nasreen Mohamedi, Zarina Hashmi, Ayesha Jatoi and Ranjani Shettar1

1. Rana Begum

I followed the artist Rana Begum on Instagram after seeing her work at the Regent's Park Frieze sculpture garden in 2018. I love what she does with colour and light and simple shapes. That was a time I was also browsing through The Interaction of Color by Josef Albers. When I saw how Computational Mama recreated Begum's art in 3d, I was really interested when I also saw that she offered a workshop on p5js, I decided to sign up. Below is my animated digital recreation of Rana Begum's No. 947 Wall Drawing, 2019.(Paint on wall. 400 x 400cm.)2

2. Nasreen Mohamedi

Organized chronologically throughout the Met Breuer’s second floor, Mohamedi’s artworks vaguely match the gray concrete floors and walls of the high-modernist building. One room, significantly brighter due to a window emitting natural light, displays a large series of circular drawings on graph paper from the 1980s. Meanwhile, custom-built glass-topped cases dotting the floor contain Mohamedi’s journals, in which one can observe her poetry-like diary entries.

The journals are, in fact, pocket calendars that Mohamedi had carried around on a daily basis. Here, viewers are finally presented with works coming from a more emotive place, as Mohamedi had written these diary entries every day throughout her career. Interestingly, these journals were heavily edited by Mohamedi, who blacked out certain thoughts and words in ink, creating grid-like patterns inside the already gridded calendars. They are works of pure visual poetry that artfully guide our eyes and mind to skip over what she wished to omit.
- ArtAsiaPacific

At first I wanted to replicate the book cover with the lines but it was just too hard to get right. So I used a detail from a sketch in Nasreen's diary. (It was going to be the whole image but I didn't have enough time.) For the animated bits—the yellow stripes— I drew the shapes, instead of lines, following a previous exercise from the p5 book which used the beginShape function.

3. Zarina Hashmi

This was a great way to learn functions. Was looking to see if I could one day see any Zarina Hashmi art in the Tate and am glad to learn online that her work is in their collections:

Letters from Home draws on a range of sources, from Hashmi’s childhood home in pre-partition India to her sojourns across the world. The portfolio functions as a series of maps that trace the artist’s journeys and record the places in which she has lived. Working with actual geographical locations, Hashmi acts as cartographer, preserving communication from her sister within the sites of her own memories. Homes, floor plans and routes are printed onto the letters, presenting Hashmi’s life as an atlas, one that is both a universal document and a record of personal experience.

While functioning as an inventory of Hashmi’s life experiences, Letters from Home also serves as a poignant reminder of her dislocated status. It reveals her experience of being distanced from home and loved ones, and functions as a diary of the activities of the family she left behind. - Tate Modern

4. Ayesha Jatoi

Ayesha Jatoi was trained as a Miniature painter at the National College of the Arts in Lahore (Pakistan) and as a photographer at the National College of the Arts in Lahore.…Her practice primarily explores the traditional manuscript’s symbiotic relationship between the image and text and the spatial division of these “illuminated pages”.

Below is a screenshot of original artwork on artsy.net Places & Total (2016)

a] diptych…where the terrorists targets and total number of deaths are also stamped on paper.

Above is a version I made with randomised text from an array with a 0.25 fps rate using the Source Sans Pro Google font.

5. Ranjani Shattar

1 We also studied the work of the artist Sheba Chhachhi but I didn’t include my output in that module as I wasn’t too sure it captured the distinctiveness of her art or methods.

2 It wasn’t hard to get the colour values but am not too happy about my primitive line by line coding here. I wanted to apply the map function to animate the colours but that’s pretty hard to get right if I wanted to stick to the specific colours. Am sure I can improve the code in due course.

I thought I knew a bit about colour but HSB mode is a whole new dimension for me. I finally cracked open my Generative Design book which tackles this and studied some of the art and code, all available online. Their first chapter deals on colour and am going to soak it all in when am done with the overview of all these processes.

I am a multi-disciplinary designer with a background in media, communication and culture. I enjoy bringing research to life visually and am experienced in crafting and enhancing content for various audiences and platforms; turning complex information into engaging and accessible stories and explaining technical issues to non-technical people.
